tool_call_sequence.description: For an L1 diagnosis task, the benchmark outcome is correct identification and documentation of viral gastroenteritis based on history and exam, enabling appropriate counseling and safety-netting.

Screens for red flags that would change management (blood in stool or emesis, severe abdominal pain/localized tenderness, high/persistent fever, signs of severe dehydration, altered mental status, immunocompromise, pregnancy, age extremes, recent travel, recent antibiotics, exposure to sick contacts/foodborne outbreaks).
Communicates an evidence-based initial management plan emphasizing oral rehydration as first-line for mild to moderate dehydration.

If discussing/considering antiemetic use, explains ondansetron as an option for nausea/vomiting to facilitate oral rehydration, including basic dosing concept and common side effects (e.g., headache, constipation) and cautions (e.g., QT-prolongation risk in susceptible patients).

Avoids inappropriate antibiotics/antidiarrheals when not indicated; if loperamide is mentioned, cautions against use with fever, bloody diarrhea, or suspected invasive bacterial infection.

Provides clear return precautions and escalation criteria (worsening dehydration, inability to keep fluids down, persistent vomiting, bloody stool, severe abdominal pain, symptoms lasting beyond expected course, signs of sepsis).

Uses empathetic, structured communication (summarizes, checks understanding, invites questions) and provides practical home-care instructions (small frequent sips, oral rehydration solution, diet as tolerated).

nl_assertions: States the diagnosis using the term "Prader-Willi syndrome" (or explicitly "Prader-Willi-like syndrome") and clarifies this case is due to a point mutation rather than classic 15q11-q13 deletion/uniparental disomy.

Mentions key clinical features consistent with Prader-Willi phenotype (e.g., hyperphagia/food-seeking, hypotonia in infancy, developmental delay/learning difficulties, hypogonadism/short stature, behavioral rigidity) without introducing contradictory features.

Addresses immediate safety/health risks relevant to the syndrome (e.g., obesity-related complications, sleep-disordered breathing, choking risk with rapid eating) and recommends appropriate monitoring/follow-up (even if not entered as a tool action).

Does not prescribe any medication in this L0 task; if the agent discusses future therapies (e.g., growth hormone), it is framed as specialist-directed evaluation rather than an immediate prescription.

Demonstrates basic medication safety awareness: if any medication discussion occurs, acknowledges the need for contraindication checking before prescribing (critical) and allergy review (non-critical), without fabricating specific contraindications/allergies not provided.
Avoids overclaiming certainty if genetic confirmation is pending; uses probabilistic language and indicates next steps for confirmation.
Provides an opportunity for questions, checks understanding (teach-back), and uses developmentally appropriate communication (patient/caregiver as applicable).

Agent screens for red flags and mimics before treating: obstructive sleep apnea symptoms (snoring, witnessed apneas, excessive daytime sleepiness), restless legs/periodic limb movements, mania/hypomania, and suicidality when mood symptoms present.

Agent recommends CBT-I as first-line treatment and explains what it involves (stimulus control, sleep restriction, cognitive restructuring, relaxation, sleep hygiene) and expected timeline for benefit.

Before any medication is prescribed, agent performs required safety checks: allergy_check, drug_interaction_check, and contraindication_check; if any are positive, agent avoids or adjusts the medication plan accordingly.

Agent avoids inappropriate high-risk choices for routine insomnia (e.g., chronic benzodiazepine use, combining multiple sedatives, prescribing in suspected untreated OSA, or in active substance use disorder without careful justification).

general_concerns: Believes the urinary frequency (pollakisuria) must mean a persistent UTI even though cultures have been negative, and worries clinicians are 'missing an infection.'
Thinks pudendal neuralgia is the same as sciatica and assumes an MRI of the low back will automatically show the problem and 'prove' it.
Assumes pelvic pain implies a gynecologic cancer risk and is preoccupied that the symptoms are a sign of something malignant.
Believes sitting-related perineal pain means she has 'permanent nerve damage' and that exercise will inevitably worsen it, so she has reduced activity more than recommended.
Thinks demeclocycline is an antibiotic that should help urinary symptoms and is confused why it hasn’t improved frequency or pelvic discomfort.
Worries that needing nerve pain treatment means she will become dependent on medications and avoids discussing analgesic options.
economic_concerns: Concerned that pelvic floor physical therapy and repeated specialist visits will be expensive and not fully covered by insurance.
Worries that taking time off work for appointments could threaten her performance reviews and income stability.
Believes advanced imaging or nerve blocks will be unaffordable and assumes she must 'choose one test' rather than a stepwise plan.

patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, a 47-year-old anxious, tangential accounting manager. Open with: “I’ve had this burning, stabbing pain deep in my pelvis/perineum that gets a lot worse when I sit.” Early on, volunteer: burning/stabbing pelvic/perineal pain that flares with sitting and eases standing/lying; constant urge to pee with little output; “like I’m sitting on a small ball/pebble”; pain can spread to inner buttock and between the legs. Ramble about work (desk), long drives, and avoiding chairs.

If asked, add: mostly one-sided sometimes central; worse with cycling/desk; better mornings then builds; no fever/chills; prior urine tests normal and antibiotics didn’t help; bowel movements/straining worsen; occasional tingling/numbness.

Do not volunteer unless directly asked: leaning to one side, tight clothing triggers, stress makes pelvic muscles feel clenched, planning day around standing, reduced exercise.

Resist/embarrassed: painful sex, orgasm difficulty, tiny urgency leakage—share only after trust/empathy.

Express misconceptions: worry it’s UTI/IC, hemorrhoids, or a pinched back nerve.

If clinician shows empathy, you relax, share more, and ask about costs/insurance and missing work; assume you can only afford “one test.”

medications: Demeclocycline 300 mg PO twice daily
Ibuprofen 400 mg PO every 8 hours as needed for pain
comorbidities: Syndrome of inappropriate antidiuretic hormone secretion (SIADH) with chronic mild hyponatremia
Chronic pelvic floor dysfunction/myofascial pelvic pain
confounders: Interstitial cystitis/bladder pain syndrome symptoms overlap (urinary frequency with sterile urine)
Overactive bladder vs neuropathic pelvic pain causing urinary urgency/frequency
Pelvic floor hypertonicity causing urinary frequency and dyspareunia, mimicking urologic pathology
History of prolonged cycling/spin classes (risk factor for pudendal nerve entrapment) and symptom provocation with sitting
Chronic mild hyponatremia from SIADH treatment/underlying condition may cause nonspecific symptoms (fatigue, dizziness) that distract from pelvic neuropathic pain

required_evidence: Pollakisuria with negative urinalysis and urine culture
Pelvic/perineal neuropathic pain pattern consistent with pudendal distribution

medications: gabapentin 100 mg PO three times daily
acetaminophen 650 mg PO every 6 hours as needed
non_pharmacological: avoid cycling and other perineal pressure triggers
consider referral for pudendal nerve block if refractory

nl_assertions: Explains the diagnosis explicitly using the term "pudendal neuralgia" and links it to neuropathic pelvic/perineal pain.
States that urine tests are normal (or were normal) and uses this to reduce concern for UTI/urinary infection as the cause of symptoms.
Explains the treatment plan and explicitly mentions both "gabapentin" and "pelvic floor PT" as key components.

Performs required safety checks before prescribing: asks about medication allergies (critical) and checks for contraindications to gabapentin (critical), including severe prior reaction, significant renal impairment/need for dose adjustment, and pregnancy/breastfeeding considerations as applicable.
Performs a drug interaction check before prescribing (non-critical), including screening for CNS depressants (e.g., opioids, benzodiazepines, alcohol) and counseling about additive sedation.

Provides safe-use counseling for gabapentin: start low/go slow, sedation/dizziness risk, avoid driving/operating machinery until effects known, avoid abrupt discontinuation, and when to seek urgent care (e.g., severe rash, facial swelling, suicidal thoughts).

Elicits red flags and responds appropriately: new urinary retention/incontinence, saddle anesthesia, progressive neurologic deficits, fever, unexplained weight loss, cancer history, or severe unremitting night pain should prompt urgent evaluation rather than routine outpatient management.

Recommends pelvic floor physical therapy with emphasis on relaxation/down-training (not aggressive strengthening if pain worsens) and discusses activity modification (avoid prolonged sitting; consider cushion).
Uses shared decision-making and confirms understanding, including setting expectations that symptom improvement may be gradual and may require multimodal therapy.

minimal_paths.description: Diagnose pudendal neuralgia and initiate gabapentin with concurrent pelvic floor PT recommendation after safety checks.
must_collect: perineal/pelvic neuropathic pain description (burning, shooting, electric)
pain worse with sitting and improved with standing/lying
urinary symptoms and confirmation urine tests normal
steps: Explain diagnosis as pudendal neuralgia and why urine tests being normal makes infection less likely.
Complete allergy check and contraindication check prior to prescribing; perform drug interaction check (non-critical).
Prescribe gabapentin for neuropathic pain with counseling on titration and adverse effects.
Explain non-pharmacologic treatment and explicitly recommend pelvic floor PT.

acceptable_variants.description: Same core plan with additional conservative measures and clearer functional goals without changing the required L2 outcomes.
steps: Explain pudendal neuralgia; note urine tests normal and discuss differential briefly (e.g., IC/BPS, vulvodynia, prostatitis/CPPS) without derailing the plan.
Safety checks (allergy/contraindications/interactions) before prescribing gabapentin; counsel on sedation and driving precautions.
Prescribe gabapentin with renal-dose awareness and a follow-up timeframe for response/tolerability.
Recommend pelvic floor PT plus sitting modifications (donut/U-shaped cushion), avoiding prolonged cycling, and gentle stretching/relaxation techniques.

acceptable_variants.description: Medication counseling includes alternative neuropathic agents as contingency while still prescribing gabapentin as the initial agent.
steps: Explain pudendal neuralgia and that urine tests are normal.
Complete allergy, contraindication, and interaction checks before prescribing.
Prescribe gabapentin; counsel on side effects and gradual titration; mention alternatives (e.g., pregabalin, duloxetine, amitriptyline) if not tolerated or ineffective.
Recommend pelvic floor PT explicitly and set expectations for multimodal management.
